body_txt: Within the past couple days or hours, something awfully strange (and strangely awful) has taken place. Maybe that Synthetic Plague , the one the government was working on, got unleashed. Or maybe a voodoo priest's spell went awry . Maybe an alien space probe broadcast a weird signal at the Earth, or fell to Earth and brought radiation with it . Maybe there's just no more room in Hell . Whatever the cause, the result is the same; the recently dead have risen, en masse , to attack, kill, and feed on the living. With each new victim they claim, their numbers swell, and no force on Earth can contain them. As society collapses, it falls to the Big Damn Heroes (or Ragtag Bunch of Misfits ) to fight their way to safety, or at least keep shooting until things blow over. The Zombie Apocalypse has arrived. While Horror is assumed to be an inherent element of any Zombie Apocalypse story, not all the terror and conflict will necessarily be due to the zombies themselves. Much of it can instead come from the reactions of the living humans involved, and how they respond to the state of fear and violent chaos brought about by the zombies. Often, the answer is "not well" . The breakdown of society, the fear that your Fire-Forged Friends could become infected and turn against you without warning, are at least as important to a zombie story as the zombies themselves, if not more so. Common to virtually all Zombie Apocalypse tales is that, regardless of the reason the zombies attack living/non-infected people, they never attack other zombies . Whether they'll attack animals other than humans varies, but it's rare for The Virus itself to affect other species, probably because it's cheaper and easier to film humans in make-up than it is to work with animals , whether trained, animatronic, or CGI. Due to the threat that zombies pose (they did just cause the apocalypse, after all), the protagonists of more serious works will be required to become very smart very quickly (though they'll still tend to be ignorant with regard to the word "zombie" itself ). Failure is often the only option in these stories; rarely will they have an ending that could be considered "happy" by typical standards, or indeed one where humanity survives as a species. Another plot staple is that things will always, always go From Bad to Worse , either due to the characters' actions or circumstances which are out of their hands, no matter how improbable they might be. Since trying to plot a zombie outbreak from Patient Zero is long and difficult, most zombie stories are set either After the End , have zombies turn very quickly, or have a significant mass of people infected all at once by some sort of bioweapon. The Militaries Are Useless trope is usually a must in such a work, if only to keep the story from ending in five minutes. If they are actually competent, they'll likely turn out to be evil as well. The civilizational collapse will also usually take place relatively quickly, over a period of days, weeks or months instead of years. This prevents society and/or the main characters from adapting, and also makes Convenient Comas somewhat plausible. In the occasion where collapse occurs in a couple of months, a nuclear submarine, aircraft carrier, or other large vehicle could realistically be expected to weather the entire outbreak start to finish in perfect isolation and safety. Characters may also assume that their portable radios have not only limitless battery supplies but infinite reception and frequency range, with local dead air assumed to indicate a completely global collapse. (Of course, the audience might not need to speculate about this, if a Spreading Disaster Map Graphic crops up in the opening credits.) Another common staple of the Zombie Apocalypse is that the zombies are often not the only or most dangerous enemy that a survivor will have to face. It's usually other survivors, power-hungry maniacs or regular-hungry people who want to attack you to get at your food and shelter . Sometimes Humans Are the Real Monsters will occur (after all, a zombie is just a degraded human!) A subtrope of Our Zombies Are Different , and a member of The Undead trope family. See Night of the Living Mooks for cases where zombies don't threaten the end of the world. See also Zombie Gait and Everything's Deader with Zombies . Raising the Steaks is what happens when humans aren't the only creatures that can be infected by The Virus . The zombie apocalypse is almost always a case of Guilt-Free Extermination War requiring that everybody be armed . And expect a healthy dose of Improbable Infant Survival ; despite a population of millions of children at any given time in any human population very few will become (visible) zombies, and the few that do will be for audience effect. Also look for an Incongruously-Dressed Zombie or two to turn up for comic relief. Contrast Friendly Zombie , who's not there to cause the apocalypse. ( Attractive Zombies also generally tend to avert this, thanks to Beauty Equals Goodness ). May result in Doctor Infected by Patient . Note that the Zombie Apocalypse trope refers to any kind of undead apocalypse, the most common traits being that the undead spread rapidly, wipe out humans primarily by eating or biting them, and are usually highly infectious — even if the undead happen to resemble vampires or yet another kind of monster more than zombies. Vampire versions of this nearly always involve Feral Vampires . If you are looking for different types of Zombie, see Our Zombies Are Different . Not to be confused with Vampire Apocalypse: The Series by Derek Gunn. Sometimes the 'zombies' might be a case of the Technically Living Zombie , but the overall narrative usually plays out the same way regardless. A Zombie Apocalypse can be considered a sort of Came Back Wrong on a very large scale. Also known as a "Zombocalypse."
